context:
Section 6 (Conclusions and Discussions), page 26 (arXiv v2 pdf pagination), near the end of the section beginning 'There is much interesting future work to do.'

However, the convergence property of Algorithm 3.1 is not known when there are infinitely many KKT points. In Example 5.2(ii), there are infinitely many KKT points that are not NEs, but Algorithm 3.1 is still able to get an NE in a few loops. If there are infinitely many KKT points that are not NEs, does Algorithm 3.1 still converge to find an NE? This question is mostly open to the authors.
title: Prove convergence of iterative KKT-point exclusion for nonconvex polynomial Nash equilibria
background_latex:
Consider a Nash equilibrium problem with \(N\) players. Player \(i\) chooses a strategy vector \(x_i\in\mathbb{R}^{n_i}\) and faces an optimization problem parameterized by the other players' strategies \(x_{-i}\):
\[
\min_{x_i\in\mathbb{R}^{n_i}} f_i(x_i,x_{-i}) \quad\text{s.t.}\quad g_{i,j}(x_i)=0\ (j\in E_i),\quad g_{i,j}(x_i)\ge 0\ (j\in I_i),
\]
where \(f_i\) is a polynomial in \(x=(x_1,\dots,x_N)\) and each constraint polynomial \(g_{i,j}\) depends only on \(x_i\). The feasible set of player \(i\) is
\[
X_i := \{x_i\in\mathbb{R}^{n_i}: g_{i,j}(x_i)=0\ (j\in E_i),\ g_{i,j}(x_i)\ge 0\ (j\in I_i)\},
\]
and the joint feasible set is \(X:=X_1\times\cdots\times X_N\subseteq\mathbb{R}^n\), \(n:=\sum_i n_i\).

A (pure-strategy) Nash equilibrium is a point \(x^*\in X\) such that for every \(i\), \(x_i^*\) is a global minimizer of player \(i\)'s problem at \(x_{-i}^*\).

Assume each constraint tuple \(g_i:=(g_{i,1},\dots,g_{i,m_i})\) is \"nonsingular\" in the sense that a certain associated polynomial matrix has full column rank over \(\mathbb{C}^{n_i}\); under this assumption, the Lagrange multipliers for the Karush–Kuhn–Tucker (KKT) conditions can be expressed as polynomial functions \(\lambda_{i,j}(x)\). Then every Nash equilibrium satisfies a polynomial system encoding the KKT equalities together with complementarity and sign conditions:
1) stationarity \(\nabla_{x_i} f_i(x) - \sum_{j=1}^{m_i} \lambda_{i,j}(x)\nabla_{x_i} g_{i,j}(x_i)=0\),
2) primal feasibility \(g_{i,j}(x_i)=0\) for \(j\in E_i\), \(g_{i,j}(x_i)\ge 0\) for \(j\in I_i\),
3) complementarity \(\lambda_{i,j}(x) g_{i,j}(x_i)=0\) for \(j\in I_i\),
4) dual feasibility \(\lambda_{i,j}(x)\ge 0\) for \(j\in I_i\).

The paper proposes an iterative algorithm (Algorithm 3.1) that repeatedly solves a polynomial optimization problem over the KKT-feasible set (using a generic strictly convex quadratic objective \([x]_1^\top\Theta [x]_1\) to pick a unique candidate) and then checks whether the candidate is a Nash equilibrium by solving each player's best-response problem. If the candidate \(u\) is not an equilibrium, the algorithm adds finitely many additional polynomial inequalities of the form
\[
f_i(v,x_{-i})-f_i(x_i,x_{-i})\ge 0
\]
for selected best-response points \(v\) (collected in sets \(K_i\)) to exclude the found non-equilibrium KKT point(s) and repeats.
problem_definition_latex:
Assume the nonsingularity condition holds so that polynomial Lagrange multiplier expressions \(\lambda_{i,j}(x)\) exist, and suppose a Nash equilibrium exists.

Analyze Algorithm 3.1 described above in the case where there are infinitely many KKT points that are not Nash equilibria. Determine whether, under such circumstances, the iterative procedure of repeatedly:
1) solving the current polynomial optimization problem over the KKT-feasible set augmented with the accumulated inequalities indexed by \(K_i\),
2) checking the resulting optimizer \(u\) for the Nash equilibrium property via each player's global best-response computation,
3) enlarging \(K_i\) by adding some best-response optimizer(s) when \(u\) fails the Nash equilibrium check,
necessarily converges (in finitely or infinitely many iterations) to a Nash equilibrium.

Equivalently: give conditions under which Algorithm 3.1 is guaranteed to find a Nash equilibrium (or certify nonexistence) even when the set of non-equilibrium KKT points is infinite, or provide an explicit example showing failure of convergence.

location_in_paper: Section 6 (Conclusions and Discussions), page 26 (arXiv v2 pdf pagination), near the end of the section beginning 'There is much interesting future work to do.'
open_problem_quote: However, the convergence property of Algorithm 3.1 is not known when there are infinitely many KKT points. In Example 5.2(ii), there are infinitely many KKT points that are not NEs, but Algorithm 3.1 is still able to get an NE in a few loops. If there are infinitely many KKT points that are not NEs, does Algorithm 3.1 still converge to find an NE? This question is mostly open to the authors.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: One can algorithmically generate small polynomial games with low degrees and dimensions and test Algorithm 3.1 computationally for cycling or divergence, but the search space is large and the outcome depends on implementation choices (e.g., which best-response optimizers are added to the sets \(K_i\)). Still, convergence or failure can often be checked empirically on finite-dimensional instances.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate counterexamples (families of polynomial games) and algorithm variants (rules for selecting points added to \(K_i\), choice of \(\Theta\), etc.) can be encoded and scored by running the resulting procedure and measuring progress toward an equilibrium or detecting nontermination. The main limitation is computational cost of repeated Moment-SOS solves and the difficulty of designing a fitness signal that correlates with provable nonconvergence rather than just slow convergence on sampled instances.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
Algorithm 3.1 of Nie--Tang (2023) reduces a nonconvex polynomial Nash equilibrium problem to a sequence of polynomial optimization problems over the KKT-feasible set, where KKT conditions are encoded purely polynomially using Lagrange multiplier expressions \(\lambda_{i,j}(x)\) available under a nonsingularity hypothesis. The algorithm selects a unique KKT candidate by minimizing a generic strictly convex quadratic \([x]_1^\top\Theta[x]_1\), then verifies the Nash property by solving each player’s global best-response problem. When verification fails, it adds finitely many best-response inequalities \(f_i(v,x_{-i})-f_i(x_i,x_{-i})\ge 0\) (with \(v\) drawn from best responses) to exclude the offending KKT point and repeats. The paper proves finite termination when the set of non-equilibrium KKT points \(G\setminus G^*\) is finite, and shows this holds generically because the KKT system has finitely many solutions.

The open difficulty is precisely the regime where \(G\setminus G^*\) is infinite: the exclusion step removes the current optimizer but may leave infinitely many other non-equilibrium KKT points with smaller \([x]_1^\top\Theta[x]_1\) value than any equilibrium, potentially causing an infinite sequence of exclusions without reaching \(G^*\). Existing foundational work on polynomial Lagrange multiplier expressions (Nie 2019) and on finite/asymptotic convergence of moment-SOS relaxations (Nie 2013, 2014) ensures that each inner polynomial optimization and best-response computation can be solved (often with finite convergence under standard optimality/compactness assumptions), but it does not by itself control the outer-loop dynamics of adding best-response cuts.

At present, there appears to be no general convergence theorem guaranteeing that the iterative KKT-point exclusion procedure must converge to a Nash equilibrium when there are infinitely many non-equilibrium KKT points. Progress likely requires additional structural conditions ensuring that the accumulated best-response inequalities eventually carve out a neighborhood of \(G^*\) (e.g., some form of error bound, isolation/finite stratification of equilibria, or a separation property between equilibria and non-equilibrium KKT points in the \(\Theta\)-objective), or else an explicit counterexample where the algorithm cycles through an infinite descending chain of non-equilibrium KKT points while equilibria remain feasible but never selected.

sources.title: Nash Equilibrium Problems of Polynomials
sources.theorem_or_result: Introduces Algorithm 3.1 for computing Nash equilibria of polynomial games by solving a sequence of polynomial optimization problems over the KKT-feasible set (with polynomial Lagrange multiplier expressions under a nonsingularity assumption) and iteratively excluding non-equilibrium KKT points via best-response inequalities. Proves finite termination when the set of non-equilibrium KKT points is finite, and shows finite termination for generic data (finitely many KKT points). Leaves open convergence when there are infinitely many non-equilibrium KKT points.
sources.connection: This is the source paper posing the open question: whether iterative KKT-point exclusion (Algorithm 3.1) converges when there are infinitely many KKT points that are not Nash equilibria; it provides the algorithmic framework, assumptions (nonsingularity giving polynomial multipliers), and the known finite-termination regime.

sources.title: Convex generalized Nash equilibrium problems and polynomial optimization
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops semidefinite relaxation methods for convex polynomial generalized Nash equilibrium problems using (rational/parametric) Lagrange multiplier expressions; guarantees finding a GNE or certifying nonexistence under convexity and regularity assumptions.
sources.connection: Provides closely related methodology and convergence guarantees in the convex setting; highlights that the difficulty in Algorithm 3.1 is specifically the nonconvex case where KKT points need not be equilibria, motivating why infinite non-equilibrium KKT sets are problematic.

sources.title: Tight relaxations for polynomial optimization and Lagrange multiplier expressions
sources.theorem_or_result: Establishes existence and construction of polynomial Lagrange multiplier expressions under nonsingularity conditions and proves tightness/finite convergence properties for SOS relaxations augmented with such expressions.
sources.connection: Algorithm 3.1 relies on polynomial multiplier expressions \(\lambda_{i,j}(x)\) to encode KKT conditions without introducing multiplier variables; this paper supplies the foundational theory ensuring these expressions exist and yield tight SOS relaxations, which is prerequisite for analyzing convergence of the outer exclusion loop.

sources.title: Optimality conditions and finite convergence of Lasserre’s hierarchy
sources.theorem_or_result: Gives optimality conditions (e.g., constraint qualification, strict complementarity, second-order sufficiency) implying finite convergence of Lasserre’s moment-SOS hierarchy for polynomial optimization.
sources.connection: Algorithm 3.1 repeatedly solves polynomial optimization problems (3.10) and best-response problems (3.8) via moment-SOS relaxations; understanding when these inner solves terminate finitely is important when analyzing the outer-loop behavior in the infinite-KKT regime.

sources.title: Certifying convergence of Lasserre’s hierarchy via flat truncation
sources.theorem_or_result: Develops the flat truncation criterion for certifying finite convergence and extracting global minimizers from moment relaxations.
sources.connection: The algorithm’s subproblems are solved by moment relaxations; flat truncation is the practical and theoretical certificate used in the source paper to detect exactness and extract candidate KKT points and best responses, affecting any rigorous convergence analysis of the overall iterative exclusion scheme.
